questions.question: What are family media practices?

options.text: Rules that only apply to children, not adults
Shared agreements and routines about how, when, and where the family uses technology together and individually
Banning all technology from the household
Allowing unlimited screen time for everyone

questions.explanation: Family media practices are shared agreements about technology use — rules, routines, and norms that apply to the whole family (not just kids) and promote healthy habits, connection, and balance.

questions.question: What does research show about parental modeling of technology use?

options.text: Parents' technology use has no impact on children's behavior
Children model their parents' technology habits — parental phone use during family time predicts children's problematic use
Children do the opposite of what parents do
Only explicit rules matter, not parental behavior

questions.explanation: Research shows children model parental behavior. Parents who use phones during meals, conversations, or family time signal that it's acceptable — undermining explicit rules. Modeling matters as much as rules.

questions.question: Which is an example of an effective family media practice?

options.text: Parents use phones freely, but children have strict limits
Family-wide 'no phones at dinner' rule, enforced for everyone including adults
No rules — everyone decides individually what works for them
All devices confiscated at 8pm except for parents

questions.explanation: Effective practices apply to the whole family (not just kids) and create shared boundaries. 'No phones at dinner for anyone' models healthy behavior and creates connection. Double standards undermine credibility.

questions.question: What does the American Academy of Pediatrics recommend regarding family media use?

options.text: Create a Family Media Plan with agreed-upon screen-free zones (meals, bedrooms) and times (before bed), balanced with other activities
Unlimited screen time as long as homework is done

questions.explanation: The AAP recommends creating a Family Media Plan with screen-free zones (meals, bedrooms), screen-free times (before bed, during family activities), and balance with physical activity, sleep, and face-to-face connection.

questions.question: If you have children or plan to, what family media practices would you want to establish? If you were creating a 'Family Media Plan' today, what would be the top 3 rules that apply to everyone (including adults)?
